slides.title: What a trigger is
body: A trigger is Apex code that runs AUTOMATICALLY when records are inserted, updated, or deleted on a specific object.
Real example: 'when a new Lead is created, auto-assign it to a sales rep' — that's a trigger. Slack's 'when someone reacts to a message, send a notification' is the same pattern in their codebase.

vocabulary.def: Apex code that fires automatically in response to database events on a specific object.

slides.title: Trigger anatomy
body: Shape: trigger TriggerName on ObjectName (events) { body }.
Events: before insert, after insert, before update, after update, before delete, after delete, after undelete. List the ones you care about.

slides.title: Your first trigger
slides.intro: Type this. Fires on every Account insert/update.
lineExplanations: Declare the trigger. Name = AccountTrigger. Object = Account. Events = before insert and before update.
Trigger.new is a List of the records being processed. Always loop it.
Mutate a field on each record. In a 'before' context, this change gets persisted automatically — no DML needed.
Close loop. Close trigger.

slides.title: before vs after
body: BEFORE: runs before the record is committed. Edits to Trigger.new persist for free, no DML needed. Use for validation and field defaulting.
AFTER: runs after the record exists in the database (with an Id). Use for related-record updates, integrations, or anything that needs the new Id.

vocabulary.def: Runs before commit. Mutate Trigger.new directly; no DML required to persist changes.
Runs after commit. Records have Ids. Use for related-record updates and integrations.

slides.title: Trigger context variables
body: Trigger.new — list of new versions of records. Trigger.old — list of old versions (update/delete only). Trigger.newMap and oldMap — same data keyed by Id for fast lookup.
Trigger.isInsert / isUpdate / isDelete / isBefore / isAfter — booleans telling you which event fired.

vocabulary.def: The list of records currently being processed (the new versions).
Map<Id, SObject> of the old versions, keyed by record Id. Available on update/delete only.

slides.title: Detecting a field change
slides.intro: Type this. Compare new vs old to see what changed.
lineExplanations: Trigger on Lead before update.
Loop the new versions.
Look up the OLD version of the same record from oldMap.
Compare new Status vs old Status.
Logic that fires only when this specific field changed.
Closes.

slides.title: The cardinal sin: SOQL/DML in a trigger loop
body: A trigger can fire on up to 200 records at once. Putting a SOQL query or DML statement inside the for-loop = up to 200 queries/statements = blown governor limit.
Real example: this is THE most common reason Apex code fails in production. Bulkification is the cure.

slides.title: Bulkification — the rescue
body: Pattern: collect Ids in the loop → ONE query AFTER the loop → second loop processes the queried data.
Real example: this is the same pattern any high-throughput pipeline uses. Stripe's webhook handlers fan out IDs, batch-fetch related data, then process.

vocabulary.def: Writing trigger code so it works correctly when the trigger fires on 200 records at once.

slides.title: Bulkified pattern
slides.intro: Type this. Collect ids first, then one query, then one DML.
lineExplanations: Trigger on Contact after insert.
Set to collect parent Account ids — Set dedupes.
First pass: walk Contacts, collect parent ids.
Skip nulls; otherwise add the AccountId to the set.
Close first loop.
ONE SOQL query after the loop using IN :acctIds bind.
ONE DML update after the loop.

slides.title: One trigger per object (best practice)
body: Define ONE trigger per SObject; let it call a separate Handler class for the actual logic. Keeps order-of-execution predictable and code testable.
Real example: every well-architected Salesforce org has AccountTriggerHandler, ContactTriggerHandler, etc. — thin trigger files dispatching to plain Apex classes.

vocabulary.def: Best-practice pattern: thin trigger delegates all logic to a regular Apex class for testability and clarity.

slides.title: Handler delegation
slides.intro: Type this. The trigger is one line per event; the handler does the work.
lineExplanations: Trigger declaration.
Branch on isInsert; delegate the records to a static handler method.
Branch on isUpdate; pass new + oldMap so the handler can detect changes.
Close trigger.

slides.front: Why does putting a SOQL query inside a for-loop in a trigger blow up in production?
slides.back: Triggers can fire on up to 200 records. Looping with a SOQL inside = up to 200 queries = past the 100-per-transaction governor limit.
slides.detail: The fix is bulkification: collect needed ids in the loop; query once AFTER the loop; process the results.

slides.question: You need to default a Lead's Source field to 'Web' when it has no value. Which trigger event?
options: before insert — mutate Trigger.new and the change persists with no extra DML.
slides.explanation: before insert. Set the field on Trigger.new and Salesforce persists the change as part of the insert — no DML needed, no governor-limit cost.

slides.title: What you now know
body: Trigger anatomy, before vs after, context variables, bulkification, and the handler-delegation pattern.
Next: Inheritance, Polymorphism & Interfaces — the OOP pillars that let classes extend, override, and substitute for one another.
